question:
Define a function $w: \mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{Z}$
as follows. For $\left| a \right|, \left| b \right| \leq 2$,
let $w(a,b)$ be as in the table shown; otherwise, let $w(a,b) = 0$.
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{|cc|r|r|r|r|r|}
\hline
\multicolumn{2}{|c|}{\multirow{2}{*}{$w(a,b)$}} & \multicolumn{5}{|c|}{$b$} \\
&  & -2 & -1 & 0 & 1 & 2 \\
\hline
& -2 & -1 & -2 & 2 & -2 & -1 \\
& -1 & -2 & 4 & -4 & 4 & -2 \\
$a$ & 0 & 2 & -4 & 12 & -4 & 2 \\
& 1 & -2 & 4 & -4 & 4 & -2 \\ 
& 2 &  -1 & -2 & 2 & -2 & -1 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
For every finite subset $S$ of $\mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z}$,
define
\[
A(S) = \sum_{(\mathbf{s}, \mathbf{s}') \in S \times S} w(\mathbf{s} - \mathbf{s}').
\]
Prove that if $S$ is any finite nonempty subset of $\mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z}$, then $A(S) > 0$.
(For example, if $S = \{(0,1), (0,2), (2,0), (3,1)\}$, then the terms in $A(S)$ are $12, 12, 12, 12, 4, 4, 0, 0, 0,0,-1,-1,-2,-2,-4,-4$.)
solution:
(by Harm Derksen)
Consider the generating functions
\begin{align*}
f(x,y) &= \sum_{(a,b) \in S} x^a y^b, \\
g(x,y) &= \sum_{(a,b) \in \mathbb{Z}^2} w(a,b) x^a y^b.
\end{align*}
Then $A(S)$ is the constant coefficient of the Laurent polynomial
$h(x,y) = f(x,y) f(x^{-1}, y^{-1}) g(x,y)$. We may compute this coefficient by averaging over unit circles:
\begin{align*}
(2 \pi)^2 A(S) &= \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{2\pi} h(e^{is}, e^{it})\,dt\,ds \\
&= \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{2\pi} \left| f(e^{is}, e^{it}) \right|^2 g(e^{is}, e^{it}) \,dt\,ds.
\end{align*}
Consequently, it is enough to check that $g(e^{is}, e^{it})$ is a nonnegative real number for all $s,t \in \mathbb{R}$. But
$g(e^{is}, e^{it}) = 16 G(\cos s,\cos t)$ for
\[
G(z,w) = zw + z^2 + w^2 - z^2 w - zw^2 - z^2w^2.
\]
If $z,w \in [-1,1]$ and $zw \geq 0$, then
\[
G(z,w) = zw(1-zw) + z^2(1-w) + w^2(1-z) \geq 0.
\]
If $z,w \in [-1,1]$ and $zw \leq 0$, then
\[
G(z,w) = (z+w)^2 - zw(1+z)(1+w) \geq 0.
\]
Hence $g(e^{is},e^{it}) \geq 0$ as desired.

descriptive_long.question:
Define a function $\weightfn: \mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{Z}$
as follows. For $\left| \firstidx \right|, \left| \secondidx \right| \leq 2$,
let $\weightfn(\firstidx,\secondidx)$ be as in the table shown; otherwise, let $\weightfn(\firstidx,\secondidx) = 0$.
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{|cc|r|r|r|r|r|}
\hline
\multicolumn{2}{|c|}{\multirow{2}{*}{$\weightfn(\firstidx,\secondidx)$}} & \multicolumn{5}{|c|}{$\secondidx$} \\
&  & -2 & -1 & 0 & 1 & 2 \\
\hline
& -2 & -1 & -2 & 2 & -2 & -1 \\
& -1 & -2 & 4 & -4 & 4 & -2 \\
$\firstidx$ & 0 & 2 & -4 & 12 & -4 & 2 \\
& 1 & -2 & 4 & -4 & 4 & -2 \\ 
& 2 &  -1 & -2 & 2 & -2 & -1 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
For every finite subset $\subsetset$ of $\mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z}$,
define
\[
\totalsum(\subsetset) = \sum_{(\mathbf{anglelong}, \mathbf{anglelong}') \in \subsetset \times \subsetset} \weightfn(\mathbf{anglelong} - \mathbf{anglelong}').
\]
Prove that if $\subsetset$ is any finite nonempty subset of $\mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z}$, then $\totalsum(\subsetset) > 0$.
(For example, if $\subsetset = \{(0,1), (0,2), (2,0), (3,1)\}$, then the terms in $\totalsum(\subsetset)$ are $12, 12, 12, 12, 4, 4, 0, 0, 0,0,-1,-1,-2,-2,-4,-4$.)
descriptive_long.solution:
(by Harm Derksen)
Consider the generating functions
\begin{align*}
\genfunone(\axisvarx,\axisvary) &= \sum_{(\firstidx,\secondidx) \in \subsetset} \axisvarx^{\firstidx} \axisvary^{\secondidx}, \\
\genfuntwo(\axisvarx,\axisvary) &= \sum_{(\firstidx,\secondidx) \in \mathbb{Z}^2} \weightfn(\firstidx,\secondidx) \axisvarx^{\firstidx} \axisvary^{\secondidx}.
\end{align*}
Then $\totalsum(\subsetset)$ is the constant coefficient of the Laurent polynomial
$\prodpoly(\axisvarx,\axisvary) = \genfunone(\axisvarx,\axisvary) \genfunone(\axisvarx^{-1}, \axisvary^{-1}) \genfuntwo(\axisvarx,\axisvary)$. We may compute this coefficient by averaging over unit circles:
\begin{align*}
(2 \pi)^2 \totalsum(\subsetset) &= \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{2\pi} \prodpoly(e^{i\anglelong}, e^{i\anglewide})\,d\anglewide\,d\anglelong \\
&= \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{2\pi} \left| \genfunone(e^{i\anglelong}, e^{i\anglewide}) \right|^2 \genfuntwo(e^{i\anglelong}, e^{i\anglewide}) \,d\anglewide\,d\anglelong.
\end{align*}
Consequently, it is enough to check that $\genfuntwo(e^{i\anglelong}, e^{i\anglewide})$ is a nonnegative real number for all $\anglelong,\anglewide \in \mathbb{R}$. But
$\genfuntwo(e^{i\anglelong}, e^{i\anglewide}) = 16 \helperfun(\cos \anglelong,\cos \anglewide)$ for
\[
\helperfun(\cosinevar,\weightfn) = \cosinevar\weightfn + \cosinevar^2 + \weightfn^2 - \cosinevar^2 \weightfn - \cosinevar \weightfn^2 - \cosinevar^2\weightfn^2.
\]
If $\cosinevar,\weightfn \in [-1,1]$ and $\cosinevar\weightfn \geq 0$, then
\[
\helperfun(\cosinevar,\weightfn) = \cosinevar\weightfn(1-\cosinevar\weightfn) + \cosinevar^2(1-\weightfn) + \weightfn^2(1-\cosinevar) \geq 0.
\]
If $\cosinevar,\weightfn \in [-1,1]$ and $\cosinevar\weightfn \leq 0$, then
\[
\helperfun(\cosinevar,\weightfn) = (\cosinevar+\weightfn)^2 - \cosinevar\weightfn(1+\cosinevar)(1+\weightfn) \geq 0.
\]
Hence $\genfuntwo(e^{i\anglelong},e^{i\anglewide}) \geq 0$ as desired.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Define a function $sandpaper: \mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{Z}$
as follows. For $\left| turntable \right|, \left| doorknob \right| \leq 2$,
let $sandpaper(turntable,doorknob)$ be as in the table shown; otherwise, let $sandpaper(turntable,doorknob) = 0$.
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{|cc|r|r|r|r|r|}
\hline
\multicolumn{2}{|c|}{\multirow{2}{*}{$sandpaper(turntable,doorknob)$}} & \multicolumn{5}{|c|}{$doorknob$} \\
&  & -2 & -1 & 0 & 1 & 2 \\
\hline
& -2 & -1 & -2 & 2 & -2 & -1 \\
& -1 & -2 & 4 & -4 & 4 & -2 \\
$turntable$ & 0 & 2 & -4 & 12 & -4 & 2 \\
& 1 & -2 & 4 & -4 & 4 & -2 \\ 
& 2 &  -1 & -2 & 2 & -2 & -1 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
For every finite subset $colosseum$ of $\mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z}$,
define
\[
raincloud(colosseum) = \sum_{(\mathbf{nightfern}, \mathbf{nightfern}') \in colosseum \times colosseum} sandpaper(\mathbf{nightfern} - \mathbf{nightfern}').
\]
Prove that if $colosseum$ is any finite nonempty subset of $\mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z}$, then $raincloud(colosseum) > 0$.
(For example, if $colosseum = \{(0,1), (0,2), (2,0), (3,1)\}$, then the terms in $raincloud(colosseum)$ are $12, 12, 12, 12, 4, 4, 0, 0, 0,0,-1,-1,-2,-2,-4,-4$.)
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
(by Harm Derksen)
Consider the generating functions
\begin{align*}
stingward(pinecrown,lighthawk) &= \sum_{(turntable,doorknob) \in colosseum} pinecrown^{turntable} lighthawk^{doorknob}, \\
marblegum(pinecrown,lighthawk) &= \sum_{(turntable,doorknob) \in \mathbb{Z}^2} sandpaper(turntable,doorknob) pinecrown^{turntable} lighthawk^{doorknob}.
\end{align*}
Then $raincloud(colosseum)$ is the constant coefficient of the Laurent polynomial
$snowglove(pinecrown,lighthawk) = stingward(pinecrown,lighthawk)\, stingward(pinecrown^{-1}, lighthawk^{-1})\, marblegum(pinecrown,lighthawk)$. We may compute this coefficient by averaging over unit circles:
\begin{align*}
(2 \pi)^2 raincloud(colosseum) &= \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{2\pi} snowglove(e^{i\,nightfern}, e^{i\,chocowire})\,dchocowire\,dnightfern \\
&= \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{2\pi} \left| stingward(e^{i\,nightfern}, e^{i\,chocowire}) \right|^2\, marblegum(e^{i\,nightfern}, e^{i\,chocowire}) \,dchocowire\,dnightfern.
\end{align*}
Consequently, it is enough to check that $marblegum(e^{i\,nightfern}, e^{i\,chocowire})$ is a nonnegative real number for all $nightfern,chocowire \in \mathbb{R}$. But
$marblegum(e^{i\,nightfern}, e^{i\,chocowire}) = 16\, driftwood(\cos nightfern,\cos chocowire)$ for
\[
driftwood(ambervine,sandpaper) = ambervine\,sandpaper + ambervine^2 + sandpaper^2 - ambervine^2 sandpaper - ambervine sandpaper^2 - ambervine^2 sandpaper^2.
\]
If $ambervine,sandpaper \in [-1,1]$ and $ambervine\,sandpaper \geq 0$, then
\[
driftwood(ambervine,sandpaper) = ambervine\,sandpaper(1-ambervine\,sandpaper) + ambervine^2(1-sandpaper) + sandpaper^2(1-ambervine) \geq 0.
\]
If $ambervine,sandpaper \in [-1,1]$ and $ambervine\,sandpaper \leq 0$, then
\[
driftwood(ambervine,sandpaper) = (ambervine+sandpaper)^2 - ambervine\,sandpaper(1+ambervine)(1+sandpaper) \geq 0.
\]
Hence $marblegum(e^{i\,nightfern},e^{i\,chocowire}) \geq 0$ as desired.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Define a function $emptiness: \mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{Z}$
as follows. For $\left| stagnant \right|, \left| frozenvar \right| \leq 2$,
let $emptiness(stagnant,frozenvar)$ be as in the table shown; otherwise, let $emptiness(stagnant,frozenvar) = 0$.
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{|cc|r|r|r|r|r|}
\hline
\multicolumn{2}{|c|}{\multirow{2}{*}{$emptiness(stagnant,frozenvar)$}} & \multicolumn{5}{|c|}{$frozenvar$} \\
&  & -2 & -1 & 0 & 1 & 2 \\
\hline
& -2 & -1 & -2 & 2 & -2 & -1 \\
& -1 & -2 & 4 & -4 & 4 & -2 \\
$stagnant$ & 0 & 2 & -4 & 12 & -4 & 2 \\
& 1 & -2 & 4 & -4 & 4 & -2 \\ 
& 2 &  -1 & -2 & 2 & -2 & -1 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
For every finite subset $wholeset$ of $\mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z}$,
define
\[
negativesum(wholeset) = \sum_{(\mathbf{s}, \mathbf{s}') \in wholeset \times wholeset} emptiness(\mathbf{s} - \mathbf{s}').
\]
Prove that if $wholeset$ is any finite nonempty subset of $\mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z}$, then $negativesum(wholeset) > 0$.
(For example, if $wholeset = \{(0,1), (0,2), (2,0), (3,1)\}$, then the terms in $negativesum(wholeset)$ are $12, 12, 12, 12, 4, 4, 0, 0, 0,0,-1,-1,-2,-2,-4,-4$.)
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
(by Harm Derksen)
Consider the generating functions
\begin{align*}
staticform(outputval,horizval) &= \sum_{(stagnant,frozenvar) \in wholeset} outputval^{stagnant} horizval^{frozenvar}, \\
stagnantmap(outputval,horizval) &= \sum_{(stagnant,frozenvar) \in \mathbb{Z}^2} emptiness(stagnant,frozenvar) outputval^{stagnant} horizval^{frozenvar}.
\end{align*}
Then $negativesum(wholeset)$ is the constant coefficient of the Laurent polynomial
$fragment(outputval,horizval) = staticform(outputval,horizval)\,staticform(outputval^{-1},horizval^{-1})\,stagnantmap(outputval,horizval)$. We may compute this coefficient by averaging over unit circles:
\begin{align*}
(2 \pi)^2\,negativesum(wholeset) &= \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{2\pi} fragment(e^{i\,lengthval}, e^{i\,widthval})\,d\!widthval\,d\!lengthval \\
&= \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{2\pi} \left| staticform(e^{i\,lengthval}, e^{i\,widthval}) \right|^2
      stagnantmap(e^{i\,lengthval}, e^{i\,widthval}) \,d\!widthval\,d\!lengthval.
\end{align*}
Consequently, it is enough to check that $stagnantmap(e^{i\,lengthval}, e^{i\,widthval})$ is a nonnegative real number for all $lengthval,widthval \in \mathbb{R}$. But
$stagnantmap(e^{i\,lengthval}, e^{i\,widthval}) = 16\,trivialmap(\cos lengthval,\cos widthval)$ for
\[
trivialmap(imagival,emptiness) = imagival\,emptiness + imagival^2 + emptiness^2 - imagival^2\,emptiness - imagival\,emptiness^2 - imagival^2\,emptiness^2.
\]
If $imagival,emptiness \in [-1,1]$ and $imagival\,emptiness \ge 0$, then
\[
trivialmap(imagival,emptiness) = imagival\,emptiness(1-imagival\,emptiness) + imagival^2(1-emptiness) + emptiness^2(1-imagival) \ge 0.
\]
If $imagival,emptiness \in [-1,1]$ and $imagival\,emptiness \le 0$, then
\[
trivialmap(imagival,emptiness) = (imagival+emptiness)^2 - imagival\,emptiness(1+imagival)(1+emptiness) \ge 0.
\]
Hence $stagnantmap(e^{i\,lengthval},e^{i\,widthval}) \ge 0$ as desired.

garbled_string.question:
Define a function $qzxwvtnp: \mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{Z}$
as follows. For $\left| hjgrksla \right|, \left| vrgploni \right| \leq 2$,
let $qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla,vrgploni)$ be as in the table shown; otherwise, let $qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla,vrgploni) = 0$.
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{|cc|r|r|r|r|r|}
\hline
\multicolumn{2}{|c|}{\multirow{2}{*}{$qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla,vrgploni)$}} & \multicolumn{5}{|c|}{$vrgploni$} \\
&  & -2 & -1 & 0 & 1 & 2 \\
\hline
& -2 & -1 & -2 & 2 & -2 & -1 \\
& -1 & -2 & 4 & -4 & 4 & -2 \\
$hjgrksla$ & 0 & 2 & -4 & 12 & -4 & 2 \\
& 1 & -2 & 4 & -4 & 4 & -2 \\ 
& 2 &  -1 & -2 & 2 & -2 & -1 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
For every finite subset $cpydrsom$ of $\mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z}$,
define
\[
mzjxcklq(cpydrsom) = \sum_{(\mathbf{radjovkn}, \mathbf{radjovkn}') \in cpydrsom \times cpydrsom} qzxwvtnp(\mathbf{radjovkn} - \mathbf{radjovkn}').
\]
Prove that if $cpydrsom$ is any finite nonempty subset of $\mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z}$, then $mzjxcklq(cpydrsom) > 0$.
(For example, if $cpydrsom = \{(0,1), (0,2), (2,0), (3,1)\}$, then the terms in $mzjxcklq(cpydrsom)$ are $12, 12, 12, 12, 4, 4, 0, 0, 0,0,-1,-1,-2,-2,-4,-4$.)
garbled_string.solution:
(by Harm Derksen)
Consider the generating functions
\begin{align*}
lhmkdvza(sbgvkilp,dftnqwer) &= \sum_{(hjgrksla,vrgploni) \in cpydrsom} sbgvkilp^{hjgrksla} dftnqwer^{vrgploni}, \\
bqtwsneo(sbgvkilp,dftnqwer) &= \sum_{(hjgrksla,vrgploni) \in \mathbb{Z}^2} qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla,vrgploni) sbgvkilp^{hjgrksla} dftnqwer^{vrgploni}.
\end{align*}
Then $mzjxcklq(cpydrsom)$ is the constant coefficient of the Laurent polynomial
$mkpvradl(sbgvkilp,dftnqwer) = lhmkdvza(sbgvkilp,dftnqwer) lhmkdvza(sbgvkilp^{-1}, dftnqwer^{-1}) bqtwsneo(sbgvkilp,dftnqwer)$. We may compute this coefficient by averaging over unit circles:
\begin{align*}
(2 \pi)^2 mzjxcklq(cpydrsom) &= \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{2\pi} mkpvradl(e^{i radjovkn}, e^{i ufqneizl})\,d ufqneizl\,d radjovkn \\
&= \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{2\pi} \left| lhmkdvza(e^{i radjovkn}, e^{i ufqneizl}) \right|^2 bqtwsneo(e^{i radjovkn}, e^{i ufqneizl}) \,d ufqneizl\,d radjovkn.
\end{align*}
Consequently, it is enough to check that $bqtwsneo(e^{i radjovkn}, e^{i ufqneizl})$ is a nonnegative real number for all $radjovkn,ufqneizl \in \mathbb{R}$. But
$bqtwsneo(e^{i radjovkn}, e^{i ufqneizl}) = 16 vclqprme(\cos radjovkn,\cos ufqneizl)$ for
\[
vclqprme(ksmbltay,qzxwvtnp) = ksmbltay qzxwvtnp + ksmbltay^{2} + qzxwvtnp^{2} - ksmbltay^{2} qzxwvtnp - ksmbltay qzxwvtnp^{2} - ksmbltay^{2} qzxwvtnp^{2}.
\]
If $ksmbltay,qzxwvtnp \in [-1,1]$ and $ksmbltay qzxwvtnp \geq 0$, then
\[
vclqprme(ksmbltay,qzxwvtnp) = ksmbltay qzxwvtnp (1-ksmbltay qzxwvtnp) + ksmbltay^{2}(1-qzxwvtnp) + qzxwvtnp^{2}(1-ksmbltay) \geq 0.
\]
If $ksmbltay,qzxwvtnp \in [-1,1]$ and $ksmbltay qzxwvtnp \leq 0$, then
\[
vclqprme(ksmbltay,qzxwvtnp) = (ksmbltay+qzxwvtnp)^{2} - ksmbltay qzxwvtnp (1+ksmbltay)(1+qzxwvtnp) \geq 0.
\]
Hence $bqtwsneo(e^{i radjovkn},e^{i ufqneizl}) \geq 0$ as desired.

kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1.  A Fourier-analytic formulation.  
For a finite S write  

f(x,y,z)=\sum _{(a,b,c)\in S} x^{a}y^{b}z^{c}  (Laurent polynomial),  

and let  

g(x,y,z)=\sum _{(a,b,c)\in \mathbb{Z}^3} w(a,b,c)x^{a}y^{b}z^{c}.  

Because convolution turns into multiplication of generating functions,  

A(S)=([x^{0}y^{0}z^{0}] f(x,y,z)f(x^{-1},y^{-1},z^{-1})g(x,y,z)).          (1)

As in the original problem, the constant coefficient can be extracted by integrating over the 3-torus:

(2\pi )^3A(S)=\int _{0}^{2\pi }\int _{0}^{2\pi }\int _{0}^{2\pi }|f(e^{i\alpha },e^{i\beta },e^{i\gamma })|^2\cdot g(e^{i\alpha },e^{i\beta },e^{i\gamma }) d\alpha  d\beta  d\gamma .        (2)

Hence A(S)>0 once we know that 

(i) g(e^{i\alpha },e^{i\beta },e^{i\gamma }) is a non-negative real number for every (\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma )\in \mathbb{R}^3;  
(ii) it is positive on a set of positive measure in the 3-torus.

Step 2.  A closed form for g on the torus.  
Introduce

\Lambda (\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma )=6-(e^{i\alpha }+e^{-i\alpha }+e^{i\beta }+e^{-i\beta }+e^{i\gamma }+e^{-i\gamma })
          =6-2( cos \alpha +cos \beta +cos \gamma  ).

Observe that \Lambda \geq 0 for all (\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma ) because each cosine is at most 1.  A direct computation using the table for w (or, faster, using that our w was engineered as a polynomial in the discrete Laplacian) shows that

g(x,y,z)=\Lambda (x,y,z)^2+\Lambda (x,y,z).                                             (3)

Indeed,

g(x,y,z)= (6-(x+x^{-1}+y+y^{-1}+z+z^{-1}))^2
        +(6-(x+x^{-1}+y+y^{-1}+z+z^{-1})),

and expanding (3) yields exactly the prescribed integer coefficients 48,-13,1,2.

Substituting x=e^{i\alpha }, y=e^{i\beta }, z=e^{i\gamma } gives

g(e^{i\alpha },e^{i\beta },e^{i\gamma })=\Lambda (\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma )\cdot (\Lambda (\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma )+1) \geq 0,                (4)

with equality only when \Lambda =0, i.e. when cos \alpha =cos \beta =cos \gamma =1, which forces (\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma )\equiv (0,0,0) mod 2\pi .

Step 3.  Strict positivity of the integral.  
Because S is finite and non-empty, f is a non-zero trigonometric (Laurent) polynomial.  Hence |f|^2 is continuous, non-negative and not identically zero, so its set of positivity has positive 3-dimensional measure.

Equation (4) tells us that g is non-negative everywhere and strictly positive except at the single point (\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma )=(0,0,0).  Therefore the integrand in (2) is non-negative everywhere and strictly positive on a set of positive measure (the intersection of ``|f|^2>0'' with ``\Lambda >0''), whence the triple integral is strictly positive.  By (2) this implies A(S)>0, completing the proof.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimension.  
The problem moves from â„¤Â² to â„¤Â³, so both the Fourier domain and the combinatorics of the kernel enlarge from a 2â€“torus to a 3â€“torus, adding an extra variable and significantly complicating any direct inspection or elementary enumeration approach.

2. Larger support and many more weights.  
Instead of a 3Ã—3 table (9 numbers), the kernel now has support on 27 lattice points, with four different weight levels (48,âˆ’13,2,1).  Simple pattern spotting is impossible; one must understand how the coefficients were produced.

3. Need for structural understanding.  
The solution hinges on recognising that w is a quadratic polynomial in the threeâ€“dimensional discrete Laplacian.  Without that observation the expansion of g and the verification of nonâ€“negativity are prohibitively long.  Participants must therefore combine discrete differential operators with Fourier analysisâ€”material usually outside routine olympiad fare.

4. Measureâ€“theoretic argument.  
To transform nonâ€“negativity of g into strict positivity of A(S) one must note that g vanishes only on a set of measure 0 and combine this with the continuity and nonâ€“triviality of |f|Â².  This step forces competitors to mix harmonic analysis with elementary measure theory, none of which was required in the original task.

5. Algebraic expansion in three variables.  
Even after guessing (3), checking that it produces exactly the stated integer weights entails manipulating Laurent polynomials in three variables; the bookkeeping is far more intricate than in the 2â€“variable case.

All of these layers make the enhanced kernel variant substantially harder than both the original problem and the previously supplied kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1.  A Fourier-analytic formulation.  
For a finite S write  

f(x,y,z)=\sum _{(a,b,c)\in S} x^{a}y^{b}z^{c}  (Laurent polynomial),  

and let  

g(x,y,z)=\sum _{(a,b,c)\in \mathbb{Z}^3} w(a,b,c)x^{a}y^{b}z^{c}.  

Because convolution turns into multiplication of generating functions,  

A(S)=([x^{0}y^{0}z^{0}] f(x,y,z)f(x^{-1},y^{-1},z^{-1})g(x,y,z)).          (1)

As in the original problem, the constant coefficient can be extracted by integrating over the 3-torus:

(2\pi )^3A(S)=\int _{0}^{2\pi }\int _{0}^{2\pi }\int _{0}^{2\pi }|f(e^{i\alpha },e^{i\beta },e^{i\gamma })|^2\cdot g(e^{i\alpha },e^{i\beta },e^{i\gamma }) d\alpha  d\beta  d\gamma .        (2)

Hence A(S)>0 once we know that 

(i) g(e^{i\alpha },e^{i\beta },e^{i\gamma }) is a non-negative real number for every (\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma )\in \mathbb{R}^3;  
(ii) it is positive on a set of positive measure in the 3-torus.

Step 2.  A closed form for g on the torus.  
Introduce

\Lambda (\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma )=6-(e^{i\alpha }+e^{-i\alpha }+e^{i\beta }+e^{-i\beta }+e^{i\gamma }+e^{-i\gamma })
          =6-2( cos \alpha +cos \beta +cos \gamma  ).

Observe that \Lambda \geq 0 for all (\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma ) because each cosine is at most 1.  A direct computation using the table for w (or, faster, using that our w was engineered as a polynomial in the discrete Laplacian) shows that

g(x,y,z)=\Lambda (x,y,z)^2+\Lambda (x,y,z).                                             (3)

Indeed,

g(x,y,z)= (6-(x+x^{-1}+y+y^{-1}+z+z^{-1}))^2
        +(6-(x+x^{-1}+y+y^{-1}+z+z^{-1})),

and expanding (3) yields exactly the prescribed integer coefficients 48,-13,1,2.

Substituting x=e^{i\alpha }, y=e^{i\beta }, z=e^{i\gamma } gives

g(e^{i\alpha },e^{i\beta },e^{i\gamma })=\Lambda (\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma )\cdot (\Lambda (\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma )+1) \geq 0,                (4)

with equality only when \Lambda =0, i.e. when cos \alpha =cos \beta =cos \gamma =1, which forces (\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma )\equiv (0,0,0) mod 2\pi .

Step 3.  Strict positivity of the integral.  
Because S is finite and non-empty, f is a non-zero trigonometric (Laurent) polynomial.  Hence |f|^2 is continuous, non-negative and not identically zero, so its set of positivity has positive 3-dimensional measure.

Equation (4) tells us that g is non-negative everywhere and strictly positive except at the single point (\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma )=(0,0,0).  Therefore the integrand in (2) is non-negative everywhere and strictly positive on a set of positive measure (the intersection of ``|f|^2>0'' with ``\Lambda >0''), whence the triple integral is strictly positive.  By (2) this implies A(S)>0, completing the proof.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimension.  
The problem moves from â„¤Â² to â„¤Â³, so both the Fourier domain and the combinatorics of the kernel enlarge from a 2â€“torus to a 3â€“torus, adding an extra variable and significantly complicating any direct inspection or elementary enumeration approach.

2. Larger support and many more weights.  
Instead of a 3Ã—3 table (9 numbers), the kernel now has support on 27 lattice points, with four different weight levels (48,âˆ’13,2,1).  Simple pattern spotting is impossible; one must understand how the coefficients were produced.

3. Need for structural understanding.  
The solution hinges on recognising that w is a quadratic polynomial in the threeâ€“dimensional discrete Laplacian.  Without that observation the expansion of g and the verification of nonâ€“negativity are prohibitively long.  Participants must therefore combine discrete differential operators with Fourier analysisâ€”material usually outside routine olympiad fare.

4. Measureâ€“theoretic argument.  
To transform nonâ€“negativity of g into strict positivity of A(S) one must note that g vanishes only on a set of measure 0 and combine this with the continuity and nonâ€“triviality of |f|Â².  This step forces competitors to mix harmonic analysis with elementary measure theory, none of which was required in the original task.

5. Algebraic expansion in three variables.  
Even after guessing (3), checking that it produces exactly the stated integer weights entails manipulating Laurent polynomials in three variables; the bookkeeping is far more intricate than in the 2â€“variable case.

All of these layers make the enhanced kernel variant substantially harder than both the original problem and the previously supplied kernel variant.
